latest.title: Block Admission application
latest.content:
Today 15:43
RNS Number : 6771K
Caledonian Holdings PLC
01 July 2026
1 July 2026
Caledonian Holdings PLC
("Caledonian" or the "Company")
Block Admission application
Caledonian Holdings PLC (AIM: CHP), the AIM quoted investing company focused on building an integrated financial services group, announces that an application has been made to the London Stock Exchange for the block admission of 26,010,702 ordinary shares of nominal value £0.01 each in the Company ("Ordinary Shares") to trading on AIM (the "Block Listing Shares"). The block admission will be in place for an initial six month period from Admission.
The Block Listing Shares comprise the first tranche of shares (the "ATM Shares") to be issued pursuant to the At the Market Facility (the "ATM Facility") established with Axis Capital Markets Limited ("Axis"), further details of which were announced on 19 June 2026 (the "ATM Announcement").
As set out in the ATM Announcement, the ATM Shares will be issued and allotted to Axis or its appointed FCA-regulated custodian in tranches. Axis will use all reasonable endeavours to sell the ATM Shares during the term of the ATM Facility, subject to the customary selling restrictions set out in the ATM Announcement. The Initial ATM Tranche will be issued fully paid and will rank
pari passu
in all respects with the Company's existing Ordinary Shares.
Application has been made to the London Stock Exchange for admission of the Block Listing Shares, to trading on AIM ("Admission"). It is expected that Admission will become effective at 8.00 a.m. on or around 2 July 2026.
Enquiries:
Caledonian Holdings PLC
Jim McColl, Executive Director
Brent Fitzpatrick, Non-Executive Chairman
Tel: +44 (0) 7950 389469
Allenby Capital Limited
(Nominated Adviser)
Nick Athanas / David Asquith
Tel: +44 (0) 20 3328 5656
AlbR Capital Limited
(Joint Broker)
Tel: +44 (0) 20 7469 0930
Axis Capital Markets Limited
(Joint Broker)
Richard Hutchison
Tel: +44 (0) 20 3026 0320
END
This information is provided by RNS, the news service of the London Stock Exchange. RNS is approved by the Financial Conduct Authority to act as a Primary Information Provider in the United Kingdom. company_30s.what_they_do: CHP.L runs a digital‑banking and e‑commerce platform that lets merchants accept payments online and offers consumers a mobile‑first banking experience. The core product is a white‑label banking service that can be embedded into retailers’ sites, plus a suite of payment tools for small‑to‑mid‑size businesses.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The company is positioned in the fast‑growing fintech‑payments niche, where traditional banks are losing market share to agile, tech‑driven solutions. By combining banking licences with an e‑commerce acquisition, CHP.L is building a vertically integrated ecosystem that could capture a share of the £200 bn UK digital‑payments market.
company_30s.current_state: CHP.L is still in the growth‑phase, raising capital to meet regulatory requirements and to fund its recent purchase of Aspire Commerce Group. The business is operating at a loss, but it has secured a regulatory capital package and is working toward a full banking licence. The share price is trading well below the company’s current valuation, reflecting the high burn and the lack of revenue.

rns_narrative: The company is busy closing several financial deals: a £25 m regulatory capital agreement, a new ATM facility, and a joint‑broker appointment that should improve liquidity and distribution. These moves signal that CHP.L is trying to shore up its balance sheet and meet licensing requirements.

Strategically, the acquisition of Aspire Commerce Group is the headline. The deal gives CHP.L a ready‑made e‑commerce customer base and a platform that can be leveraged for cross‑selling banking products.

Corporate governance updates—AGM notices, share consolidation, and a new investment director—indicate an effort to streamline operations and reassure shareholders that the company is moving toward a clear exit strategy.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Quiet hope – investors are still silent but the few bullish posts hint at a potential turnaround once the banking licence is granted.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: A handful of traders point to the company’s undervaluation and the fact that a full banking licence could unlock a £100 m valuation. They see the Aspire deal as a platform for rapid revenue growth.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Critics worry about the heavy cash burn, the uncertainty of regulatory approval, and the fact that the company has yet to post any earnings. They fear a prolonged capital‑raising cycle could wipe out the share price.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: Sentiment has moved from indifferent to cautiously optimistic after the capital‑raising announcements, but the overall mood remains muted.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends is flat – there is no public buzz, so a news event could deliver a surprise move.

near_term_catalysts: Regulatory approval for the full banking licence – this would open up a new revenue stream and boost credibility.
Completion of the Aspire Commerce Group integration – a first‑quarter revenue report would validate the acquisition.
A positive earnings release or a cash‑flow improvement statement that shows the company is moving toward break‑even.
A significant volume spike on a key support level that signals capitulation has finished.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a 3‑day volume surge that exceeds the 20‑day moving‑average volume, a price breakout above the 50‑day simple moving average, and any press release about the banking licence or revenue numbers.
expect_next.timing_regime: The share has already fallen deep (over 85 % from its peak). The APEX score suggests a tight compression, so we are likely in the early stages of a mean‑reversion cycle. A breakout above the 50‑day SMA would confirm that the bottom is being tested.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: The stock is trading far below its intrinsic value. If the bank licence is granted and revenue starts to materialise, a bounce to the 50‑% recovery level would give a 200 % upside from the current price. The deep crash (≈90 %) is a classic setup for a large asymmetric gain.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: If the licence is delayed or the Aspire integration stalls, the company could keep burning cash for another year, pushing the price down to a new, lower trough. A worst‑case scenario is a 20 % drop from the current price if the market loses confidence in the licensing timeline.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential loss is capped at the current share price; upside could be 2‑3 times that if the stock recovers to halfway levels. The asymmetry is clear: a small downside versus a large upside.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter when the price closes above the 50‑day SMA with a volume spike of at least 1.5× the 20‑day average. Size the position to 10–15 % of the portfolio, set a stop 10 % below the entry level, and trail the stop to lock in gains as the price climbs.
playbook.if_watching: Hold a small position until the licence is announced or until revenue appears. If either event occurs, move to a full position using the same entry criteria.
playbook.if_passing: None – there are no imminent delisting or bankruptcy signals. The risk is limited to a further decline in the current price.

ai_insights.bottom_line: CHP.L sits on a deep, 90 % crash, but recent capital‑raising and a strategic e‑commerce acquisition point to an imminent catalyst; the asymmetry favors a contrarian bullish stance with a clear upside if the banking licence materialises.
